QF.id: XIII Introduction to Forwards and Futures_12

question.text: A company holds a portfolio of equity stocks worth Rs 50 lakhs with a beta of 1.2 relative to NIFTY. How many NIFTY futures contracts should they sell to hedge? (NIFTY at 25,000, lot size 25)

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Number of futures = (Portfolio value × Beta) / (Index level × Lot size) = (50,00,000 × 1.2) / (25,000 × 25) = 60,00,000 / 6,25,000 = 9.6 ≈ 10 contracts. Beta adjusts because a high-beta portfolio moves more than the index. **The Trap:** 8 contracts ignores beta. 2 contracts uses wrong lot size. 12 contracts uses beta incorrectly. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series VIII Ch3 covers beta-based hedging. Compare with duration-based hedging for bonds (Series IV) and notional-based hedging for currencies (Series I). **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Beta hedging is an approximation — actual hedge effectiveness depends on how stable the stock-index beta relationship is during the hedge period.
